perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall mean: (a) any data or information that is supplied by either Party (which will be deemed to include, in the case of the Company, any data or information with respect to any Member of the Company supplied by the Company or such Member to the Investor in connection with the operation of the Company or the performance of any obligation, or the exercise of any rights, under the LLC Agreement or any Related Agreement), including by its Affiliates (the "DISCLOSING PARTY"), or the agents, employees, authorized representatives or nominees thereof (such persons being referred to collectively as the REPRESENTATIVES") to the other Party including its Affiliates (the "RECEIVING PARTY") (or the Representatives of the Receiving Party) including, but not limited to, information regarding: products and services planning, marketing strategies, strategic and business plans, finance, operations, customer relationships, customer profiles, sales estimates and internal performance results relating to the past, present or future business activities of the Disclosing Party and its owners, customers, clients and suppliers
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall mean: (a) any data or information that is supplied by either Party (which will be deemed to include, in the case of the Company, any data or information with respect to any Member of the Company supplied by the Company or such Member to the Investor in connection with the operation of the Company or the performance of any obligation, or the exercise of any rights, under the LLC Agreement or any Related Agreement), including by its Affiliates (the "DISCLOSING PARTY"), or the agents, employees, authorized representatives or nominees thereof (such persons being referred to collectively as the REPRESENTATIVES") to the other Party including its Affiliates (the "RECEIVING PARTY") (or the Representatives of the Receiving Party) including, but not limited to, information regarding: products and services planning, marketing strategies, strategic and business plans, finance, operations, customer relationships, customer profiles, sales estimates and internal performance results relating to the past, present or future business activities of the Disclosing Party, but excluding trade secrets
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding "trade secrets" from the definition of "Confidential Information", this modification contradicts the standard legal understanding and protection afforded to trade secrets under Delaware law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA) defines and protects trade secrets. By excluding them from the definition of "Confidential Information" in the contract, the contract undermines the statutory protection afforded to trade secrets, potentially making it more difficult to enforce confidentiality obligations related to such information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 Authenticated PDF Â§ 2001 Â§ 2002 Â§ 2003 Â§ 2004 Â§ 2005 Â§ 2006 Â§ 2007 Â§ 2008 Â§ 2009 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE II Other Laws Relating to Commerce and Trade CHAPTER 20. Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes "trade secrets" from the definition of "Confidential Information." This contradicts the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), as trade secrets are a specific type of information that is legally protected. Snippet 1 defines "Trade secret" as "information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value... from not being generally known". By excluding them from the definition of Confidential Information, the contract diminishes their protection. Snippet 2 reinforces the importance of trade secrets by referencing Delaware Code Chapter 20, which is dedicated to them. The altered contract language directly undermines the protections provided by DUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall (i) destroy all memoranda, notes, records, drawings, manuals, other documents or materials and any other manifestation (and all copies thereof) pertaining to or arising from the Confidential Information in any form or medium (whether written, typewritten, recorded, electronic or otherwise) and (ii) at the option of the Receiving Party, either return to the Disclosing Party or destroy the Confidential Information supplied by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives. Upon the return and/or destruction of such materials, the Receiving Party agrees to certify, in writing, that all of the foregoing materials have been destroyed or surrendered to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall, if it deems it reasonable, destroy all memoranda, notes, records, drawings, manuals, other documents or materials and any other manifestation (and all copies thereof) pertaining to or arising from the Confidential Information in any form or medium (whether written, typewritten, recorded, electronic or otherwise) and (ii) at the option of the Receiving Party, either return to the Disclosing Party or destroy the Confidential Information supplied by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives. Upon the return and/or destruction of such materials, the Receiving Party agrees to certify, in writing, that all of the foregoing materials have been destroyed or surrendered to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'if it deems it reasonable' grants the Receiving Party discretion in destroying confidential information, potentially conflicting with legal obligations to protect trade secrets. The duty to protect trade secrets is not discretionary under Delaware law.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the destruction of confidential information discretionary, the modified text weakens the ability of the Disclosing Party to prevent misappropriation and obtain injunctive relief, thus conflicting with DUTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces discretion ('if it deems it reasonable') in the Receiving Party's obligation to destroy confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws. Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, and Snippet 1 also defines 'Improper means' as 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By allowing the Receiving Party to decide if destruction is 'reasonable', the changed text weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to prevent misappropriation, because it creates a situation where destruction is not mandatory and could be deemed unreasonable by the receiving party even if it would prevent misappropriation. This directly undermines the duty to maintain secrecy, which is a core component of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: The obligation of confidentiality and restriction on use in Section 2 hereof shall not apply to any Confidential Information that the Receiving Party proves: (a) was in the public domain prior to the date of this Agreement or subsequently came into the public domain other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party; (b) was received by the Receiving Party from a third party free of any obligation of confidence of the Receiving Party to such third party; provided that such third party is not known by the Receiving Party, after reasonable diligence, to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the Disclosing Party; (c) was already in the possession of or known to the Receiving Party, without obligation of confidentiality, prior to receipt thereof, directly or indirectly, from the Disclosing Party; (d) is requested to be disclosed in a judicial or administrative law, regulation or proceeding after all reasonable legal remedies for maintaining such information in confidence have been exhausted, including, but not limited to, giving the Disclosing Party reasonable advance notice of the potential for such disclosure and allowing the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order concerning such disclosure; or (e) is subsequently and independently developed by employees, consultants or agents of the Receiving Party without reference to the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligation of confidentiality and restriction on use in Section 2 hereof shall not apply to any Confidential Information that the Receiving Party proves: (a) was in the public domain prior to the date of this Agreement or subsequently came into the public domain; (b) was received by the Receiving Party from a third party; (c) was already in the possession of or known to the Receiving Party; (d) is requested to be disclosed in a judicial or administrative law, regulation or proceeding; or (e) is subsequently and independently developed by employees, consultants or agents of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'obligation of confidence' clauses and the 'reasonable diligence' requirement substantially weakens the protection of confidential information, particularly trade secrets. This is against the spirit of the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA defines misappropriation partly based on the knowledge or reason to know that the information was a trade secret. Removing the requirement for 'reasonable diligence' to ascertain if a third party is bound by confidentiality significantly lowers the standard for the Receiving Party, making it easier to claim they didn't know the information was protected, directly contradicting the intent of DUTSA to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA). The original text included 'reasonable diligence' for the Receiving Party to determine if a third party was bound by confidentiality. The changed text removes this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means.' By removing the 'reasonable diligence' requirement, the receiving party can more easily claim they didn't 'know or have reason to know' the information was a trade secret, undermining DUTSA's intent to protect trade secrets. This makes the contradiction direct and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be terminated except following Withdrawal by the Investor pursuant to the terms of the LLC Agreement. The obligations of confidentiality and limitations on use under this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the execution of this Agreement; PROVIDED, CONFIDENTIAL Page 29 <Page> HOWEVER, notwithstanding the term of this Agreement, any provisions hereof that by their nature extend beyond such term or termination shall remain in effect beyond such term or termination under this Section 6 until fulfilled and shall apply to either Party's successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated at any time by either party with 30 days written notice. The obligations of confidentiality and limitations on use under this Agreement shall not survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By making the agreement terminable at will and eliminating the survival clause for confidentiality, the modification directly contradicts the implied duty to protect trade secrets under Delaware law. The 5 year survival clause is also removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA provides for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. Removing the survival clause allows for potential misappropriation after termination without recourse, directly undermining the protection afforded by DUTSA. The implied duty of good faith and fair dealing also suggests that parties cannot simply terminate agreements to then exploit confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts Delaware trade secret law by removing the survival clause for confidentiality and making the agreement terminable at will, thus enabling potential misappropriation of trade secrets after termination without recourse. This undermines the protection offered by the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent' and 'Improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By removing the survival clause and allowing termination at will, the changed text facilitates potential breaches of the duty to maintain secrecy, directly conflicting with the provisions outlined in the statute for protecting trade secrets. The original agreement's 5 year survival clause aligns with the protection of trade secrets, while its removal allows for the potential misuse of confidential information post-termination, a clear contradiction.
